title: Dueling Contests and Platform’s Coordinating Role
abstract: Crowdsourcing platforms typically take a passive approach, and they let the competing firms freely design their own contests and allow every solver to self-select and join any of the concurrently running contests. In a model of competing noise-driven contests, we show that the duopoly prize allocation has fewer (but larger) prizes compared with a monopolist contest designer. We also find that contests with firm-chosen budgets and solvers’ endogenous participation create coordination inefficiencies. Thus, platform policies that constrain the competing firms from freely choosing their budgets and offer solvers non-enforceable recommendations toward specific noise-driven contests strictly enhance total welfare. Extending our framework to include arbitrarily correlated ability-driven contests, we highlight the critical role of inter-contest dependence on the efficacy of a platform’s interventions. Specifically, platform nudges to improve solver-contest (mis)matches are welfare enhancing only when the contests are sufficiently related, and allowing solvers to self-sort is appropriate otherwise.

author.Konstantinos I. Stouras: Michael Smurfit School of Business, University College Dublin, Dublin A94 XF34, Ireland
author.Sanjiv Erat: Rady School of Management, University of California, San Diego, La Jolla, California 92093

article.1. Introduction: Crowdsourced innovation usually takes place on platforms where multiple firms compete to attract a broad pool of solvers in exchange for prizes. In many, if not most, of these settings, the platform provider takes a hands-off “laissez faire” approach and relies on solvers to choose and participate in contests that are attractive to them. For example, Wazoku Crowd (previously InnoCentive), one of the largest marketplaces for innovation contests, has hundreds of partners that announce prizes and regularly run contests for ideation or for marketing-, technology-, healthcare-, or manufacturing-related problems. Solvers on Wazoku Crowd scan through the available contests and select which (if any) of the contests they want to participate in. Despite the widespread use of contests on platforms for real-world domains, aspects of contest design on platforms, including equilibrium incentives (that emerge from the competition between contest-organizing firms), are poorly understood outside the case of the monopolistic stand-alone contest designer. Perhaps most importantly from a practical perspective, it remains an open question whether a platform and/or the participants may stand to gain from the platform taking a more active role (compared with being a hands-off “market maker”). This paper aims to fill these gaps by studying the following research questions. How do solvers choose to participate and exert effort among competing contests, and what is the resultant equilibrium prize allocation (when each firm optimally allocates its budget to balance the solver participation-effort incentives)? Would a contest organizer offer different prizes under duopoly compared with the case when she acts as a monopolist? How do the outcomes and overall welfare compare between the case where the platform takes a hands-off approach relative to a (marginally) more active approach where the platform constrains all the contest organizers from freely setting their prize budgets and/or nudges each solver toward specific contests by offering non-enforceable recommendations? In specific, can such platform policies/actions improve outcomes and (solver and overall) welfare? We model duopolistic competition between firms that conduct innovation contests on a platform. Solvers can noncooperatively self-select which (if any) contest to submit a solution to and can further exert costly effort to enhance the quality of their submission. We distinguish betweennoise-drivenandability-drivencontests. Noise-driven contests represent the one extreme where firms seek a solution to an open-ended problem, and hence, the quality of a solver’s solution can only be decided by the respective firm ex post its evaluation. By contrast, ability-driven contests model the other extreme of innovation problems with well-defined objectives. For both settings, we study the symmetric equilibria where solvers may probabilistically enter into each contest. Our equilibrium analysis enriches our understanding of optimal contest design in two important respects: first, from the descriptive standpoint of understanding the induced participation equilibrium (among the solvers) and the equilibrium prize allocations (among the competing firms) and second, from a normative perspective of offering platform-level prescriptions that can potentially reduce (any) inefficiencies (of the identified equilibrium) and consequently, improve overall welfare. We highlight here the contribution of the current research along these two dimensions. Analysis of the induced solver equilibrium under noise-driven contests reveals that in general, depending on the characteristics of the noise distribution, firms may offermultiple identicalprizes in equilibrium. Further, the equilibrium number of such prizes under duopoly is weakly less than under monopoly (i.e., dueling firms offer (weakly) fewer and larger prizes compared with a monopolist). We also establish that when the contest noise has an increasing failure rate (IFR), then a single winner-takes-all (WTA) prize is a (strictly) dominant strategy for each firm under monopoly (and thus, under duopoly as well). By contrast, a noise-driven monopolist contest with a decreasing failure rate (DFR) should optimally evenly allocate its budget among all but the last solver, whereas offering a single WTA prize can be optimal under competition. For the large class of contests in which a firm’s objective and the nature of the innovation problem require stronger participation incentives (as opposed to effort incentives), namely contests with sufficiently large dependence on output uncertainty, we show that the WTA allocation is an approximate best response to any competing allocations. Thus, one can use a single first prize to guide the actual contest design foranynoise distribution (irrespective of the budgets and prize allocations chosen by competitors). Perhaps most importantly from a practical and normative perspective, our analysis also considers the platform’s role in managing crowdsourcing incentives for firms and solvers. On the firm side, when the platform can choose the contest budget, we find that contests with heterogeneous budgets harm both solvers and firms. Although it appears intuitive that increasing firm competition via different budgets might benefit the solvers (but would negatively impact the firms), a platform with budget-homogeneous firms induces the maximum spread of solvers across contests, and hence, it benefits both the solvers (as they expect fewer competitors per contest) and the firms in aggregate. Restricting firms’ budget choices is frequently observed in practice (e.g., when the platform recommends that firms choose their reward budget among a few available choices so that contests appear ex ante similarly attractive to solvers) (see Online Appendix EC.1). Our framework suggests that such platform policies are indeed welfare enhancing. On the solver side, we consider a setting where the platform offers “nudges” (recommendations) to the solvers. Our analysis reveals that the platform can create nudges that are self-reinforcing in that solvers have no incentive to deviate away from the platform’s recommendations. More importantly, our welfare calculations show that the solvers and the competing firms are all better off in the nudged equilibrium compared with the case where solvers self-select into contests (without platform nudges). Thus, providing the solvers with recommendations, such as by periodically highlighting a contest to a subset of solvers, improves overall welfare. Further, we study a duopoly with ability-driven heterogeneous contests in which solvers privately know their contest-dependent abilities before choosing which contest to enter. No mixed-entry strategies emerge in equilibrium in this setting because there is only a zero measure of types that are indifferent between the contests: that is, the types on a curve we characterize as a solution to a functional integro-differential equation. Hence, a reduction in a firm’s budget causes a downward shift of the whole graph of the indifference curve, affecting the entry decisions of solver types significantly different from those previously close to it. Surprisingly, despite this qualitative difference, setting equal budgets among ability-differing contests balances not only the spread of solvers per contest but also the ability distribution of the types that enter per contest. Similar to noise-driven contests, when two ability-driven contests are related (and thus, need similar solver capabilities), platform nudges that recommend each contest to a randomly chosen half of the solver population coordinate solvers’ selfish entry choices toward an (asymmetric) equilibrium and enhance overall welfare. Before proceeding further, we offer a fully worked-out example of two competing innovation contests to build the reader’s intuition on how dueling contests affect solvers’ equilibrium participation and on the platform’s coordinating role in aligning the incentives of firms and solvers. Consider a crowdsourcing platform composed of two firms andn= 4 solvers. Each firmj(j=1,2)has a budgetbj(withb1≥b2) and organizes a WTA innovation contest seeking a novel solution to an open-ended problem. The assumption of the WTA prize structure, although made here to keep the example concise, is in fact the equilibrium prize structure (seeTheorem 2for details). Thus, the two firms form aduopolyas they compete for solvers who, without coordination, can self-select to submit a solution to one of the two contests. We assume that, upon submitting, the quality of a solution is only determined ex post by its evaluation, and the ex ante quality follows the distributionF(·)with supportS⊆R. We can show that there is a symmetric equilibrium in which each solver enters contest 1 (respectively, contest 2) with probabilityp1(respectively,p2=1−p1), which equates his expected utility per contest. This equilibrium satisfiesp2<p1∈(12,1)(i.e., solvers enter “more” into the contest with higher ex ante potential because of the budget difference). Interpreting any non-entrant as an entrant that loses with certainty, we can represent thesubmittedsolution quality in each contestj(j=1,2)as if it is distributed according to theeffective quality distribution Such a modification keeps the number of participants per contestjfixed atn, regardless ofpj. Observe that for each contestj,F(z)≤Fj(z;pj)for allz∈S(with a strict inequality unlessb1=b2). Because of this stochastic dominance, the presence of firm 2 makes firm 1 face aworsedistribution of submissions relative to being a monopolist, and thus, the expected best submitted solution is lower than the expected best solution under monopoly. Suppose now that the platform suggests that both firms have equal budgets. In this case, solvers participate in both contests with equal probabilityp*=12. This induces an identical effective quality distribution per contest equal to Becausep1∈(12,1), we obtain the orderingF1(z;p1)<F*(z;12)<F2(z;p2)for allz. We can show that the aggregate welfare,π(p1)≔π1(p1)+π2(1−p1), is (strictly) concave inp1. Then, setting equal budgets makes firm 1 obtain a worse quality distribution (compared with the duopoly withb1>b2), but by concavity, this welfare loss is more than offset by the welfare gain because of the improved quality distribution obtained by firm 2. Thus, contests with homogeneous budgets strictly enhance welfare. Finally, suppose thatb1=b2=12and that the platform recommends contest 1 to any two solvers and contest 2 to the remaining two solvers (“nudged entry”). Note that following the platform’s nudge and entering the respective contest with certainty are an (asymmetric) equilibrium. Specifically, no solver has an incentive to deviate and enter a non-recommended contest because such a deviation would result in a strictly lower payoff(=13·12<12·12=14). In the case where solvers self-select contests (“blind entry”), each solver’s payoff is given by12(1−(12)4)+12(1−(12)4)4=14−(12)6, whereas in the “nudged” equilibrium, the solver’s payoff is given by12·12=14. Thus, nudged entry strictly improves solvers’ payoffs. In the remainder of the paper, we generalize the above core insights to account for a duopoly of budget-heterogeneous contests and a solver population with arbitrary size, the fact that the solution quality might depend on the solver’s ability, and the possibility that solvers can exert effort to enhance their solution quality. InSection 3, we formulate the model (with the platform serving only as a “market maker”), andSection 4characterizes the equilibrium prize allocations and associated solver entry of dueling contests for open-ended tasks (i.e., noise-driven quality). A closed-form solution for the equilibrium in this setting is intractable. We deal with this by using order statistics techniques, and we show that a firm’s problem under duopoly can be transformed into a linear program of a suitably defined monopolist, the solution of which can be readily obtained. This allows us to characterize the equilibrium prize allocation under duopoly and show that it is generally different from the optimal prizes set by a monopolist or a central planner. InSection 5, we extend our analysis by explicitly considering a platform that can recommend the budget to contest-designing firms and can nudge the solvers toward particular contests. We characterize the welfare-optimal budget and contest selection, and we contrast them with the baseline case where firms and solvers interact without any platform interference. We extend this analysis inSection 6, where we study the equilibrium and the efficacy of platform’s interventions under a duopoly of heterogeneous and arbitrarily correlated, ability-driven contests and ex ante heterogeneous solvers with privately known and contest-dependent abilities. All proofs are relegated to the Online Appendix.
article.2. Related Literature: To the best of our knowledge,Azmat and Möller (2009)is the only other paper that examines (firm-level) equilibria among competing concurrent contests. Azmat and Möller (2009)study the equilibrium of two competing Tullock contests with identical budgets and a population of ex ante identical solvers who learn the number of participants upon selecting a contest (but before choosing their effort).Azmat and Möller (2009)prove theexistenceof a symmetric equilibrium in which each identical contest awards a single WTA prize to maximize aggregate effort; unlike ours, their proof technique does not establish (prize) equilibrium uniqueness and cannot be generalized to dueling contests with heterogeneous budgets. These authors note that their (exogenously) imposed Tullock contest success function (Tullock 1980) fails to predict solvers’ expected winning quality when it is affected by some stochastic mapping of effort (seeAzmat and Möller 2009, p. 765). We bridge this gap and formulate a tractable analytical framework, in which solvers select among multiple dueling contests with non-identical budgets that offer several prizes, whereas the contest winning probability depends on solver effort and on a noise term drawn from a (general) distribution. In addition to characterizing the equilibrium prize allocation, we study the platform’s coordinating role in guiding firms’ budgets and improving the efficiency of solver entry. DiPalantino and Vojnovic (2009)studysolverequilibrium across multiple competing WTA contests by considering solvers with a privately knownglobalability across contests (which varies per solver). InDiPalantino and Vojnovic (2009, section 5), they further observe that a suitable large-scale limit ofindependentheterogeneous contests would induce the same solver participation.Bidders’ equilibrium is studied byAshlagi et al. (2011)in a duopoly of second-price auctions with identical objects (which is similar to competing contests with a global ability).DiPalantino and Vojnovic (2009)andAshlagi et al. (2011)prove the existence of a unique symmetric equilibrium in which there is always a set of solver types that mix across contests. By contrast, we allow solvers to have arbitrarily correlated and contest-dependent abilities and find that solver entry is “pure almost everywhere” in the sense that the set of solver types that randomize in equilibrium has a measure of zero. Further, we can cast the global-ability setups ofDiPalantino and Vojnovic (2009)andAshlagi et al. (2011)as limiting cases of our framework (see Online Appendix EC.4). Our paper is also related toGrossmann (2021), who considers asinglecontest organizer that can merge parallel Tullock contests. Assuming a continuum of solvers,Grossmann (2021)compares the equilibrium outcomes of parallel contests when all solvers participate in all contests (multiple participation) with the equilibrium outcomes of parallel contests when solvers can self-select their contest (single participation) and examines the question of when (if at all) solver participation needs to be restricted; see alsoKörpeoğlu et al. (2022)for a similar “contest portfolio” setting. In these papers, solvers self-select which contest to participate in givenfixedprize allocations, whereas we study the prize allocation that emergesin equilibriumamong competing innovation contests. The prior crowdsourcing literature has largely examined the role of prizes to raise the level of solvereffortand has largely neglected the entry decisions of the solvers (see, e.g.,Lazear and Rosen 1981,Nalebuff and Stiglitz 1983,Moldovanu and Sela 2001,Vojnović 2016,Chawla et al. 2019). Models of a single noise-driven contest show that a WTA prize is optimal when all solvers participate with certainty and the noise is logistically or uniformly distributed (Kalra and Shi 2001) or more generally, when solver entry is guaranteed and the noise distribution is log concave or increasing (Ales et al. 2017). In contrast, when solver participation is not guaranteed, bothKalra and Shi (2001)andAles et al. (2017)claim that multiple prizes are optimal. Multiple prizes may also be justified in noise-driven monopolistic contests because of risk aversion (Kalra and Shi 2001), search incentives (Erat and Krishnan 2012), log-convex noise distribution (Ales et al. 2017), or dynamic information revelation (Bimpikis et al. 2019); seeVojnović (2016)for a comprehensive review. We re-examine the role of prizes and the shape of the noise distribution in balancing solvers’ participation and effort incentives when solvers can self-select whether to participate in a contest (if at all). A few papers analyze contest design and solver entry in asingle(monopolistic) innovation contest.Taylor (1995)andFullerton and McAfee (1999)focus on a WTA innovation contest with complete information and argue in favor of regulating solver entry by charging appropriate entry fees that mount to the revenue of the firm.Erat and Krishnan (2012)study the design of a system of searchers who can coordinate toward finding the best solution to a complex problem.Stouras et al. (2022)endogenize participation in a monopolistic innovation contest with costly entry and incomplete information, and they show that WTA can be optimal when the firm’s budget or the contest participation cost is low, whereas multiple (unequal) prizes are optimal otherwise. Recently,Khorasani et al. (2023)studied the optimal entry level that a monopolist contest would set via screening of submissions. We extend this literature by studying the platform’s role in inducing efficient participation and whether the WTA allocation can emerge when multiple contests compete for solvers.
article.3. The Model: We consider a platform with two1contest-posting firms and a population ofn≥2solvers. Each firmj= 1, 2 is resource-constrained by a budgetbjwithb1≥b2>0. To incentivize solver submissions, each firmjsimultaneously announces a prize allocationrj=(r(1)j,r(2)j,…,r(n)j), wherer(k)jis thekth highest prize of contestj. We focus on feasible allocations (i.e.,r(1)j≥r(2)j≥⋯≥r(n)j≥0with∑k=1nr(k)j≤bjfor each firmj). Firms’ prizes induce adownstreamcontest game among solvers competing for prizes. In this game, each solveri∈[n]≔{1,…,n}simultaneously selects (at most) one contestjto participate in and how much efforteij≥0to exert toward preparing a submission. The cost of effortc(·)is assumed to be a strictly increasing, strictly convex, and twice differentiable function withc(0)=c′(0)=0andc″(·)uniformly bounded. A solver’s effort in a given contest is nonobservable by the respective firm. Thequalityof a submission by solveriin contestjis given by the random variable Thesensitivityparameterϑ≥0is a contest characteristic that captures the extent that invested effort drives submission quality, and we define00≔1. The random variableZijsupported onS⊆R≥0is assumed to follow a distribution with cumulative distribution function (CDF)F(·)and has a continuously differentiable probability density functionfwithf(·)>0andf′(·)uniformly bounded. In addition, we shall also assume thatZij, which represents heterogeneity in submission quality, is independently drawn across solvers (i.e., for each contestj,Zijis independent ofZkjfork≠i). Depending on the time at which each solver learnsZij, we distinguish among two types of contests2—noise-drivencontests andability-drivencontests. In noise-driven contests, each solver learnsZijex post choosing to exert effort in contestj. This assumption is justified for open-ended problems seeking novel solutions where winning does not just depend on how much effort is exerted or even how capable the solvers are. Rather, there is substantial uncertainty stemming from the nature of the problem itself: for example, on how best to even approach the challenge posted by the contest organizer or on how many possible approaches there are and the feasibility and ultimate potential of each approach. More broadly, in noise-driven contests, the quality of a submission is highly uncertain and cannot be reasonably deduced by the solvers before exerting their efforts. By contrast, in ability-driven contests, each solver learnsZijex ante choosing to participate and exert effort in contestj. This assumption is better suited for well-defined problems composed of tasks that require diverse specialized skills. Solver ability (or expertise) is the key driver of winning such contests (as opposed to effort or noise). For example, TopCoder runs multiple simultaneous contests for which one needs to be an expert to even be able to create a valid submission on data science, programming, or design. For each contestj, the participant with the highest quality wins the first prize,r(1)j; the participant with the second highest quality wins the second prize,r(2)j; and so on and so forth. That is, theutilityof solveriis given byr(k)j−c(eij)ifiparticipates injandXijis rankedkth highest among those who participate intoj; solvers receive zero if they do not participate in any contest. Firms’ prizes also induce anupstreamcontest game among firms competing for solvers. In this game, each firmjdetermines its budget allocation for prizes in order to maximize theexpected value of the best participating quality less the total prizes paid out(given solvers’ downstream participation-effort actions that maximize their expected utilities). The dueling contest game is modeled as a contract equilibrium with the following sequence of events. First, each competing firm simultaneously chooses its budget allocation into prizes, and then, the firm announces and commits to it. Second, solvers simultaneously and noncooperatively decide which contest to participate in and how much effort to exert in it without learning3the number of its participants. Third, each solver receives a prize corresponding to the rank order of his submission quality. Being closely related to each other, we first analyze competing noise-driven contests. After that, we return to ability-driven contests inSection 6.
article.4. Noise-Driven Dueling Contests: 4.1. Solvers’ Contest Choice and Effort Decisions In this section, we focus on noise-driven contests and study solvers’ behavior given any arbitrary budget allocations offered by the firms. Although we focus on a duopoly of dueling contests, all of our results for noise-driven contests hold more generally for anoligopolyofm≥2contests (see the Online Appendix). For simplicity, we use a transformation of variables to their logarithms in our analysis, settingZij≔logZij,eij≔logeij, andXij≔logXijso that we consider anadditive model of qualityXij=ϑ·eij+Zij, whereZij∼F(·)=F(exp(·))with associated cost functionc(eij)≔(c°exp)(eij)=c(eij). That is, the additive noise model is a one-to-one transformation of our original multiplicative noise model. Also, we denote the(n−j)th lowest out ofn− 1 order statistics CDF and density ofFbyFn−j:n−1andFn−j:n−1, respectively (see Online Appendix EC.2), and for eachk∈[n]and probabilityp, we letω(k;n,p)≔(n−1k−1)pk−1(1−p)n−k. The following proposition fully characterizes solvers’ symmetric entry equilibrium with pure-strategy effort and provides a sufficient condition for its existence. (Solver Participation-Effort Equilibrium).For any arbitrary feasible prize allocations chosen by the firms,a symmetric equilibrium with pure-strategy solver effort exists for allϑ∈[0,ϑ*],whereϑ*>0depends only on the contests’primitives(and not on the prize allocations).In such an equilibrium,ifr(1)1≥r(1)2,there exists a uniquep*∈[12,1]such that solvers participate in contest1 (respectively, 2)with probabilityp1=p*(respectively,p2=1−p*),wherep*is the unique solution to the equation Proposition 1reveals several structural properties of the solver participation-effort equilibrium in noise-driven contests that we discuss as follows. For any arbitrary prize allocations offered at the upstream firm-level game, a unique symmetric equilibrium exists in the downstream solver-level game, provided that contest effort sensitivity is sufficiently small.4In equilibrium, solvers can enter both contests with a positive probability, and they expect to earn the same expected surplus in each contest in which they participate. The equilibrium effort differs per contest, and it is a strictly increasing function of contest sensitivity and a linear combination of each contest prize weighted over each possible realization of the number of participants (seeEquation (3)). Clearly, when thenth (and lowest) prize of the first contest exceeds the first (and largest) prize of the second contest, all solvers enter into the first contest with probability 1. In such a case, we recover as special cases the equilibrium effort expressions of the monopolistic contest literature (Lazear and Rosen 1981,Kalra and Shi 2001,Ales et al. 2017,Hu and Wang 2021). 4.2. Equilibrium in Prize Allocations In this section, we study the firms’ competition when hosting their innovation contests on a crowdsourcing platform. In particular, we examine the equilibrium prize structure when budget-heterogeneous firms strategically determine their prizes to affect solvers’ self-selection and effort. For simplicity and also, for practical reasons, to analyze the upstream game among competing firms, we focus onpurestrategies in prize allocations. As a useful benchmark, we first characterize the optimal prize allocation of a monopolistic noise-driven contest. As suggested byProposition 1, this also arises endogenously in our model when a firm has a large-enough budget that effectively attracts all the market of solvers. Leth(z;F)≔f(z)1−F(z)denote thefailure rate(or hazard rate) of a distributionF. Then,Fis said to have an IFR ifh(·)is increasing and a DFR ifh(·;F)is decreasing. (Clearly, becauseF(·)≔F(exp(·)), we have thath(·;F)follows the same monotonicity withh(·;F).) Many distributions, such as the uniform, Gumbel, and Normal distributions, belong to the IFR class, whereas DFR distributions include heavy-tailed distributions, such as the Weibull, Pareto, or lognormal distributions. (Optimal Prizes of a Monopolistic Noise-Driven Contest).In a monopoly,for allϑ∈(0,ϑ*],a firm optimally allocates its entire budget evenly over In the absence of solvers’ outside options or any alternative co-existing contests, all solvers participate with certainty in the monopolistic contest. Thus, a monopoly firm focuses entirely on effort incentives when designing its noise-driven contest. Naturally, as noise impacts solver submission quality ex post exerting effort,Theorem 1finds that the shape of the noise distribution determines the optimal prize allocation at equilibrium. These insights are in agreement with the extant literature on monopolistic contests (e.g.,Kalra and Shi 2001,Terwiesch and Xu 2008,Ales et al. 2017,Bimpikis et al. 2019). Interestingly,Theorem 1shows that in a noise-driven contest, the firm must optimally split its budget evenly over multiple prizes ofequalsize. Intuitively, in a single noise-driven contest, the firm should not discriminate among ex ante homogeneous and risk-neutral solvers. By contrast, it is well known thatunequalprizes are optimal for effort incentives of ex ante heterogeneous solvers (see, e.g.,Moldovanu and Sela 2001; Stouras et al.2020,2022) or for solvers with varying risk preferences (Kalra and Shi 2001). Settingp*≔1inProposition 1and by the convexity of the cost of effort, we have that for each contestj, solver equilibrium effort strictly increases in∑ℓ=1nψℓ,nr(ℓ)j. Further, the coefficientsψℓ,ncan be expressed as differences between consecutive order statistics densities. Utilizing this structure, in the Online Appendix, we transform the monopolistic contest design of firmjto the equivalent formulation: The solution of the linear program (4) requires maximizing thenormalized prize differencesd(ℓ)jin the positionℓ*defined asℓmono*∈argmax1≤ℓ≤n1ℓ∫Sfn−ℓ:n−1(x)dF(x). This is implemented by allocating equal prizes up to theℓ*th-highest quality and no prizes to lower ranks. Clearly, offering a positive prize to thenth rank disincentivizes effort, which implies thatℓmono*≤n−1in general. Further, it is interesting, albeit perhaps intuitive, to note that an increase in a firm’s budget under monopoly will not alter the structure of the optimal allocation; the firm will optimally allocate the same number of prizesℓmono*with a higher prize offered to each rank. Theorem 1also shows that a sufficient condition for the optimality of a single WTA prize (respectively, multiple prizes) is an increasing (respectively, decreasing) failure rate of the noise distribution.5This relies on a statistical result about the monotonicity of differences of order statistics. Recall that if a distribution is convex, then it belongs to the IFR class, but being DFR implies concavity. As a consequence, we show in the Online Appendix that if the noise distribution has an IFR, then WTA is optimal. Conversely, contests with a DFR noise distribution would require relatively “flat” incentives to mitigate the large uncertainty of the noise present in the heavy tails and optimally allocaten− 1 prizes. In contrast to the monopolistic case, duopolistic competition between firms designing their own contest on the platform makes each firm face an inherentparticipation-effort trade-off.It is unclear a priori whether solver participation and effort incentives are optimized at the same prize allocation and if not, whether a firm should offer prizes to prioritize incentives for participation over effort incentives. We now turn to understand the equilibrium prize allocation under duopolistic competition between firms hosting contests. (Equilibrium in Prize Allocations).In a duopoly,for allϑ∈[0,ϑ*]and noise distributions F,there exists an equilibrium prize allocation withℓduo*prizes of equal size(defined in the Online Appendix),whereℓduo*∈{1,…,n−1}depending on ϑ and F.Further,for all F andϑ>0,we have thatℓduo*≤ℓmono*,whereas for all F whenϑ=0,we have thatℓduo*=1(WTA). Theorem 2shows that a symmetric (pure) equilibrium exists in the upstream game such that noise-driven innovation contests split their budgetevenlyamongmultipleprizes, even if their respective budgets do differ. That is, the symmetric structure of the optimal monopolistic contest is robust under competition. In equilibrium, a duopoly of competing contests behaves qualitatively similar with two separate and unrelated contests. In particular, whenϑ>0(i.e., effort does affect contest outcomes), each firm strategically chooses to ignore the presence of its competitor and prioritize solvereffortincentives over incentives for participation. To understand the underlying reason, it is insightful to consider the following noise distribution for each firmj= 1, 2 and respective solver entry probabilitypj: The functionsFjuniquely determine firms’ objectives and solvers’ participation and effort. We refer to theFjas theeffective noise distribution in contest jas it is the CDF of theparticipatingsolvers’ noise values in contestjwith the interpretation that a non-entrant is an entrant who loses with certainty6(i.e., he is an entrant having noise equal to the lower bound of the support of the noise). Using the effective noise distribution (5), the duopolistic equilibrium in prize allocations boils down to the optimal contest design of a suitably defined monopolist. Indeed, each duopolistic firmjobtains the same expected profit with a monopolist facingnsolvers and noise distributed according toFj. Similarly, in each contestj, each participating solveribehaves as if there is a monopolistic contest withn− 1 other solvers and noise distributionFj. Hence, given any arbitrary dueling prize allocations,Theorem 1applies and gives the best response prizes of firmj(subject to solvers’ effort incentive compatibility and participation constraints). Theorem 2shows that under duopoly, firms face a worse noise distribution and should allocate aweakly lower numberof prizes compared with a monopoly (and thus, thevalueof each prize will be weakly higher to exhaust the budget available). For instance, suppose that there is a single monopolist firm 1 organizing a noise-driven contest with budgetb1. Firm 1 facesnsolvers and noise distributed fromF. Suppose now that there is a competitor firm 2 organizing a contest with a weakly lower budgetb2≤b1. The presence of a competitor gives solvers an outside option to consider before determining how much effort to exert in the contest hosted by firm 1. From (5), it follows thatF(z)≤F1(z;p1)for eachz∈S; that is,Ffirst order stochastically dominatesF1. In this sense, the existence of firm 2 makes firm 1 face a lower distribution of submissions relative to being a monopolist, thus lowering firm 1’s profit. Given that lower realizations of participants become more likely under competition, all else equal, each firm should not increase the number of prizes they offer (compared with the monopoly case). This implies that whenever WTA is optimal for a firm under monopoly, then offering a single WTA prize is a (strictly) dominant strategy for the same firm in a duopoly of competing prize allocations. Hence, if we consider an oligopoly of firms with non-identical budgets and (possibly different) IFR noise distributions, then (WTA, WTA … WTA) is theuniqueequilibrium in prize allocations. Our analytical framework integrates and extends prior results that focused on exogenously specified noise distributions or solvers’ winning probabilities in monopolistic settings with no outside options. Specifically, IFR noise distributions, such as the Gumbel distribution, the Normal distribution, and the uniform distribution, have been frequently used in the literature (see, e.g.,McFadden 1974,Kalra and Shi 2001,Terwiesch and Xu 2008). Also, combining the results ofLuce (1959)andMcFadden (1974), we have that Tullock contests (Tullock 1980) can be considered as tournaments with Gumbel noise (seeVojnović 2016,Stouras 2017); thus,Theorem 2implies the WTA result ofAzmat and Möller (2009). More generally,Baye and Hoppe (2003)show that rent-seeking, patent-race, and research tournament models are all strategically equivalent to tournaments with Gumbel noise, and hence, a single prize is optimal in all of these seemingly disconnected literature streams. Our results add to this literature by establishing the robustness of the WTA allocation under competition. To illustrate the insights ofTheorem 2and contrast them withTheorem 1, we offer the following simple example. (Monopoly versus Duopoly Prizes). Consider a single innovation contest with budgetb1=0.7, effort sensitivityϑ=0.15,n= 4 solvers, and contest transformed noiseZfollowing the DFR Weibull(0.51,1,0)distribution withF(z)=1−exp(−z0.51), z > 0. Under monopoly, such a firm should optimally offermmono*=3equal prizes according to the allocationsr1*=r2*=r3*=0.73andr4*=0to obtain optimal profitπmono*=4.83. Suppose now that there is a competitor with a lower budgetb2=0.3and the same sensitivityϑand transformed noise distributionF. In the unique duopolistic equilibrium, each firm allocates its entire budget as a single WTA prize (i.e.,m1,duo*=m2,duo*=1); seeTable 1. In this equilibrium, solvers participate in contest 1 with probabilityp1=0.82, and firms obtain profits7π1,duo*=4.07<πmono*andπ2,duo*=1.19, respectively. Theorem 2further shows that allocating a single WTA prize is a strictly dominant strategy for a purely noise-driven contest (i.e., whenϑj=0for an arbitrary firmj). Indeed, in a purely noise-driven dueling contest, solvers have no incentives to exert effort, and hence, the respective firm determines its prizes8so as to maximize participation in own contest. For an intuition, suppose that firmjchooses arbitrary feasible (and nonzero) prizesr(1)j,r(2)j,…,r(n)j. Then, for any realized number of entrantsk∈[n], a participant injwins theℓth highest prizer(ℓ)jwith probability1k(which does not depend onℓ; i.e., he expects total revenue equal to1k∑ℓ=1kr(ℓ)j). Yet, by the monotonicity of the prizes, we have that the value of the first prize strictly dominates therunning average prize(i.e.,r(1)j>1k∑ℓ=1kr(ℓ)junlessk= 1). Thus, whenϑj=0, firmjmust offer a single WTA prizeirrespectiveof the characteristics of its competitors. As a consequence, by continuity with respect toϑ,for all noise distributions, sufficiently noise-driven competing contests have incentives to unilaterally deviate from the single WTA prize that are at mostεfor some small factorε>0(see Proposition EC.2 in the Online Appendix for a formal proof). In other words,Theorem 2implies that firms engaging in crowdsourcing for highly novel solutions are approximately better off awarding their entire budget to the contest winner, and this approximation becomes better with the degree that contest outcomes are driven by solution uncertainty9(as opposed to solver effort). Table 1.Duopolistic Profits ofExample 2in Which Each Firmj(j=1,2)Splits Its Respective BudgetbjEvenly intomjPrizes (of Sizebjmj) Table 1.Duopolistic Profits ofExample 2in Which Each Firmj(j=1,2)Splits Its Respective BudgetbjEvenly intomjPrizes (of Sizebjmj)
article.5. Platform’s Coordinating Role: When budget-heterogeneous firms compete for solvers, does equilibrium entry ensure that each contest attracts the “right” number of solvers? Additionally, can the platform take actions to ensure that firms’ and solvers’ interests are more aligned? Solvers’ self-sorting to contests clearly serves a self-regulating purpose. Yet, firms with low budgets might stand no chance of luring away solvers from stronger competitors. In this section, we extend our framework to investigate the platform’s coordinating role. The platform can play an active role in guiding firm and solver actions. In particular, the platform can offer budget recommendations to firms designing their innovation contests, and it can also influence solver participation by displaying a given contest more prominently than others. In fact, crowdsourcing platforms, such as 99designs, Witmart, or Kaggle, present firms with a restricted menu of budgets to choose from, and they regularly promote “featured” contests at the top of their website to draw solvers’ attention to contests (see Online Appendix EC.1). In the interest of parsimony, we follow the setup of the prior section and consider WTA dueling contests that are purely noise-driven. Because solver surplus and firms’ objectives are continuous inϑ, we can focus on the limiting caseϑ=0, whereas our results remain valid as long asϑis sufficiently small. In this setting, letpj*(j=1,2)be solver equilibrium entry probability into thejth contest indexed by their respective budget rank order. We define the expected aggregate social welfareswas the total sum of solvers’ expected utilities and firms’ profits:sw≔∑i=1nui+∑j=1mπj. 5.1. Regulating Firm Heterogeneity (Welfare-Optimal Budgets for Noise-Driven Contests).The overall welfare is uniquely maximized at the uniform entry distribution:(p1opt,p2opt)=(12,12).This is implemented in equilibrium, if and only if, the firms have equal budgets:b1=b2. The theorem shows that, on aggregate, the solvers and the firms are harmed by heterogeneous budgets. This holds in general for an arbitrary number of competing firms. The presence of budget differences between firms makes solvers cluster toward contests with larger budgets (as they offer a higher ex ante potential). Thus, contests with sufficiently small budgets are left without any entrants. Transferring the budget from higher-ranked contests toward lower-ranked contests increases the entry probability into the lower-ranked contest. Thus, the latter contest obtains adominatingeffective noise distribution and a higher submitted quality, whereas the opposite holds for the higher-ranked contest. The strict monotonicity and concavity of total welfare with respect to the realized number of entrants per contest ensure that the welfare gains more than offset the losses. Thus, platform welfare is maximized when all contests are budget homogeneous. Interestingly, in this case, solvers enter both contests with a positive probability, and hence, thespreadof solvers across contests is also maximized. Although the platform can improve overall welfare by regulating the budgets of competing firms, in the next section we move on to investigate whether the platform can further improve crowdsourcing efficiency by guiding the actions of competingsolversas well. 5.2. Nudging Solvers into Featured Contests Suppose that firms have identical10budgets normalized to unity. In this case, solvers enter each contest with an equal probabilityp*=12, and each contest expectsn¯≔n·p*=n2entrants. We denote byu*=u(p*,n)each solver’s expected payoff per contest in equilibrium: Similarly, each firm’s expected profit in equilibrium,π*=π(p*,n), is given by the expected best participant less the WTA budget paid out when at least one solver participates: The platform’s expected total welfare is the sum of all expected gains: Clearly, nudgingnj≔n¯solvers to participate in each contestj with certaintyis a relevant benchmark that corresponds to the welfare-optimal number of entrants per contest. We are interested in comparingsw(p*,n)andu(p*,n)when0<p*=n¯n<1andn>n¯(blind entry) versus whenp*=1andn=n¯(nudged entry). Blind entry corresponds to the symmetric equilibrium entry found earlier where solvers self-select into contests without coordination, whereas nudged entry corresponds to coordinated entry in which each contest receives an equal number of entrants and faces no participation uncertainty. Note that the expected number of participants is the same in both cases. We summarize our results in the following theorem. (Nudging Solvers to Noise-Driven Contests).For all noise distributions F and for every solver population sizen<∞,nudged entry strictly improves total welfare and each solver’s surplus. Theorem 4(a)explains how nudges or lack thereof affect total welfare. By running a contest, each firm spends all its budget as a WTA prize when it attracts at leastoneparticipant. At the same time, for each contest, on aggregate, solvers obtain its respective prize when at least one solver enters into that contest. That is, a firm’s budget cost is fully transferred to solvers, and total welfare simplifies to the total benefits from the best submission per contest. Clearly, the best submission is concave in the number of submissions. All else equal, it trivially follows from Jensen’s inequality that all solvers and firms in aggregate should prefer to have a certainty ofn2participants per contest compared withn2participants on expectation. Thus, it is intuitive that nudging solver entry is beneficial to total welfare. What is less straightforward is the effect of solvers’ self-selection on the solvers themselves. To understand the intuition on the benefits of nudges for each solver’s surplus, consider the number of competitors that a solver will encounter if he chooses to enter contest 1. Obviously, each of the remainingn− 1 solvers can be present with probability12. Thus, on expectation, the prize will be shared between1+n−12=n+12solvers. In contrast, when the entry is nudged, the prize is always shared betweenn2solvers. Thus,Theorem 4(b)emerges because with blind entry, there is excessive competition between solvers11(on average). In the analysis, it might appear that we implicitly assume that solvers who are “nudged” into a contest stay on the contest. However, the following argument shows that this is not the case and that solvers following the nudge are indeed an (asymmetric) equilibrium. The contest that a solver has been nudged to hasn2−1other solvers; if he jumps to a different contest, then he will be competing againstn2other solvers, and thus, he will receive a strictly lower payoff. It is interesting to note that the platform is serving a coordination role in avoiding mismatches between the solvers and the contests. Obviously, any other means that the solvers use to coordinate their entry choices will work as well. As an example, consider a correlation device that randomly picksn2numbers from one ton. Each solver choosing contest 1 if his index is in the randomly chosen set and choosing contest 2 otherwise is asymmetric correlated equilibriumthat is identical to the equilibrium in the nudged setting. Thus, from a technical perspective,Theorem 4(b)relies on the platform’s coordinating role that allows solvers to avoid excessive competition.
article.6. Coordinating Heterogeneous Solvers an: Our results in the previous section emphasize that if solvers are ex ante homogeneous, they tend to inefficiently self-select into contests. This creates excessive competition among solvers and in turn, harms welfare. Still, if solvers have heterogeneous abilities, it is feasible that self-selection by itself could be adequate to avoid such mismatches (because solvers might self-select into the appropriate contest) without the platform having to take a more proactive role. We examine this setting in the current section and address the question of whether nudging solvers into contests improves welfare in the presence of heterogeneous and contest-dependent abilities. Throughout the section, we shall consider two contests having budgetsb1,b2withb1≥b2>0, and we equip each solver with a two-dimensional12private type. In particular, each solveri∈[n]has avector of abilitieszi≔(zi1,zi2), where the privately knownzijrepresents13solveri’s realized ability in contestj= 1,  2. We suppose that the ability vectorziis drawn from an absolutely continuous, atomless, and commonly known joint probability distributionF(·,·)with densityf(·,·)over[0,1]2and that ability vectors for different solvers are drawn independently from each other. Importantly, our general joint distributionFallows solver inter-contest abilities to bearbitrarily correlated(although abilities between solvers are uncorrelated). To examine the platform’s coordinating role with heterogeneous contests within a parsimonious model, we follow the setup ofSection 5and consider WTA dueling contests that are purely ability-driven. That is, we assume thatϑ=0such thatxi, the quality vector ofi, is equal tozi. As contest quality is continuous inϑ, we can focus on the limiting caseϑ=0, whereas all of our results remain valid as long asϑis sufficiently small (see Online Appendix EC.3, where we study contests withϑ>0and formally verify this claim). Upon learning his own realized ability vector, each solver simultaneously chooses at most one contest to participate in, and we normalize solver outside option to zero. The participant with the highest ability in each contest (if any) receives a WTA prize equal to the respective contest budget; otherwise, no prize is awarded, and the contest budget is kept by the respective firm. The profit of each competing firm is equal to the highest participating ability (if any) accounting for the prize paid out, and the total welfare equals the aggregate surplus of all solvers and firms. When contests are differentiated so that solvers differ in their abilities to compete in each contest, it is not clear a priori what the best strategy for each potential participant would look like. Because we allow solver abilities per contest to be arbitrarily correlated, a high ability in one contest would imply high chances to win if a solver chooses to enter that contest. Yet, such a solver may prefer to participate in the other contest as he may reasonably expect to encounter fewer competitors or because the respective prize is higher. By restricting our attention to symmetric threshold14strategies, we are able to obtain the following result. (Symmetric Equilibrium with Contest-Dependent Abilities).Consider an arbitrary solver i with ability vectorzi=(zi1,zi2)∈[0,1]2.There exists a symmetric equilibrium in pure strategies such that solver i participates in contest1whenzi1≥γ(zi2)and participates in contest2otherwise,whereγ(·)is the solution to the functional integro-differential equation It is intuitive that a solver’s choice to enter a contest should be affected not just by his ability in the contest but also by his ability in the other contest.Theorem 5shows that in equilibrium, solvers do not simply enter the contest in which they themselves have the highest ability. Rather, they compare their ability in the more lucrative contest with theirγ-transformed ability in the lower-budget contest, whereγ(·)is an increasing function that solves (6) and implicitly captures their chance of meeting a stronger opponent, the interdependencies of skills in the contests, the budget difference, and the population size. To see the logic, consider a “marginal” solver with contest ability vector(z1,z2)=(γ(z),z), and suppose that each of his opponents self-selects contests according to the aforementioned strategy. If the marginal solver chooses to enter contest 1, he winsb1when each of his competitors with arbitrary contest ability vector(z1j,z2j)either (a) enters contest 1 and has lowerz1j-ability than him or (b) does not enter contest 1.Figure 1, left panel shows event (a) in black and event (b) in gray. The marginal solver wins contest 1 when each of his competitor has ability types sampled from the rectangle area{(z1,z2):0≤z1≤γ(z),0≤z2≤z}or the remaining gray area{(z1,z2):0≤z1≤γ(z2),z≤z2≤1}: Similarly, the marginal solver wins contest 2 when each of his competitors has ability types sampled from the shaded area ofFigure 1, right panel. Taking the complementary event, the marginal solver wins contest 2 with probability equal to Because of symmetry, the marginal solver has the same chances to win against any of hisn− 1 competitors. Thus, at equilibrium, the marginal solver is indifferent between the two contests whenb1w1(z)n−1=b2w2(z)n−1(i.e., whenw1(z)=(b2b1)1n−1w2(z)). Differentiating with respect tozgives rise toEquation (6). If we setz= 0 in this equation, we find that the area under theγ-curve is equal to The left-hand side (LHS) of (9) is the ex ante probabilityp2that an arbitrary solver participates in contest 2 (and solvers participate into contest 1 with ex ante probabilityp1=1−p2=11+ϕ∈[12,1]becauseb1≥b2).Figure 2represents the equilibrium probability mass of solvers who participate in each contest. Interestingly, these probabilities are not affected by the distribution of contest abilities (althoughγand the LHS of (9) do). Hence, despite potential contest-dependent heterogeneity in abilities, in equilibrium, firms expect aconstantfraction of participants that only depends on the budget discrepancy and the size of solver population. Before delving into the role of nudging with heterogeneous solvers and contests, we briefly discuss the uniqueness of the equilibrium.Theorem 5shows the existence and uniqueness of an entry equilibrium in symmetric pure strategies. In the Online Appendix, we further show that all mixed strategy equilibria of our game are “pure almost everywhere” in the sense that the set of solver types that randomize in equilibrium has a measure of zero. However, there also exist multiple asymmetric equilibria. For example, whenn= 2, one solver enters only one contest and his competitor enters the other contest can also be an equilibrium. We assume that coordination of this variety is unlikely to occur, at least not without the active intervention of the platform. Our equilibrium characterization for a general joint distribution of abilities has limited tractability. To further understand the equilibrium structure, we give a special case in which we can explicitly solveEquation (6). Assume that solver abilities in each contest have quantilesQ1andQ2with joint probability distribution given by the bivariate Ali–Mikhail–Haqδ-parametrized family of copulasC(q1,q2;δ)≔q1·q21−δ·(1−q1)·(1−q2)forδ∈[−1,1]. Negative (respectively, positive) values ofδmeasure negative (respectively, positive) dependence, whereasδ= 0 corresponds to the case where contest abilities are independent. In this case,Equation (6)becomesγ′(z)z(1−δ(1−z))=ϕγ(z)(1−δ(1−γ(z)))with boundary conditionγ(1)=ϕ. This has the unique solution Because there is no closed-form expression for solvers’ equilibrium in general, in what follows we focus onsymmetricdueling contests in the sense that their joint ability density is symmetric15(i.e.,f(z1,z2)=f(z2,z1)for all(z1,z2)∈[0,1]2). Under this condition,16we shall see that when firms choose the same budget, thenEquation (6)has the unique solutionγ(a)≔a, which greatly simplifies the analysis. For eachz1∈[0,1], we denote byF1(z1)the probability that another arbitrary solver either enters contest 1 with a lower ability toz1or enters contest 2. This is equal to the measure of the black and gray shaded areas in the left panel ofFigure 1. Therefore, by denoting byγ−1the inverse function ofγ, we obtain Similarly, for eachz2∈[0,1], we denote byF2(z2)the probability that another arbitrary solver either enters contest 2 with a lower ability toz2or enters contest 1. This is equal to the measure of the shaded area in the right panel ofFigure 1. Therefore, we obtain We refer to the functionFjas theeffective ability distributionin contestj(j=1,2). Working with the effective ability distribution keeps the number of solvers per contest fixed atnas it treats nonparticipants in that respective contest as participants with zero ability, and it circumvents the need to deal with randomness with respect to the number of participating solvers (seeAshlagi et al. 2011, where the same technique is applied in competing auctions). The formulation reveals that each dueling contestjobtains the same expected best participating abilities as a monopolist facingnsolvers with abilities distributed according to the newly definedFj. Each firm enjoys a heterogeneous (and privately known) value from the respective contest that it hosts on the platform. Naturally, this makes each firm willing to select a different budget for its contest to maximize its own respective objective. At the same time, because contest prizes differ, solvers may over-enter contests with higher ex ante potential (driven by a higher budget) and under-enter the rest. Our next result investigates the efficiency of the firms’ budget heterogeneity by comparing the choice of budgets that maximize aggregate platform welfare with an arbitrary feasible budget allocation. (Welfare-Optimal Budgets for Ability-Heterogeneous Contests).The platform’s aggregate welfare is maximized when firms choose the same budgetsb1=b2. Theorem 6shows that even with ex ante heterogeneous solvers, mandating no budget differentiation is welfare enhancing in crowdsourcing contests! To see the logic, consider two contests with the same budget, and suppose we raise the budget of contest 1. This causes a downward shift of the entire indifference boundaryγcreating an area between the diagonal and theγ-curve. The latter area corresponds to the additional mass of solvers who now enter contest 1 and hence, raises its respective profit. At the same time, contest 2 loses solver mass from the aforementioned area, and its respective profit decreases. However, note that the increase in profit of contest 1 comes by increasing the likelihood that more solvers oflowerabilities enter (if they exist).Theorem 6shows that the net effect of heterogeneous budgets on welfare is negative. This can be avoided by requiring equal budgets across contests so that they appear ex ante similarly attractive to solvers. In this case, similar to noise-driven contests, the spread of thenumberof solvers across contests is maximized (seeSection 5.1). Additionally, for ability-driven contests, welfare is optimal when the spread of solverabilityacross contests is maximized as well. When contest budgets are equal, the fixed point equationγ(z)=zsolvesEquation (6), and in the unique symmetric equilibrium, each solver enters only into the contest in which he has the highest ability. Then, becauseϕ=(b2b1)1n−1=1, solvers enter contests with equal ex ante probabilitiesp1*=p2*=12, and both effective ability distributionsF1andF2coincide withF*, where That is, in equilibrium with identical budgets, solvers behave as if their ability is drawn from a common “pooled” distributionP[max{Z1,Z2}≤z]for both contests as opposed to the contest-dependent marginals ofF(·,·). Further, observe that the effective ability distributions are stochastically ordered: That is, when the two contests have the same budgets, the originally lower-budget contest 2 would obtain a stochastically better distribution of abilities, and the opposite holds for the originally higher-budget contest 1. Yet,Theorem 6suggests that enforcing equal budgets is always enhancing the aggregate welfare. With respect to platform nudges, although for noise-driven contests,Theorem 4shows that solver surplus and total welfare are enhanced by nudged entry, the same is not always true for ability-driven contests. Specifically, if we consider the same type of nudges where the platform allows solvers to enter only into particular contests, then because the platform is unaware of solver abilities, it is feasible that it may misallocate the solvers and may create inefficiencies17in the solver-contest pairings. Does this inefficiency (arising from blind nudges) negate the value of spreading the solvers more evenly among the contests? Stated differently, in ability-driven contests, is the solvers’ self-sorting to contests adequate to lead to an efficient outcome without the need for the platform to do anything? Our next result answers this question, and it illustrates an interesting contingency when platform nudges still play a welfare-enhancing role. (Nudging Heterogeneous Solvers to Heterogeneous Contests).For all ability distributionsF(·,·)and for every solver population sizen<∞,nudged entry strictly improves each solver’s surplus and strictly improves total welfare if solver abilities are sufficiently correlated across contests. Nudging solver entry is beneficial for the solvers on aggregate as long as there is participation uncertainty in each contest. For solver populations with a finite size, equilibrium entry may leave some contests unattended so that each contest’s budget is never fully allocated to solvers on aggregate. Hence, the platform’s nudges serve as a way to coordinate the solvers’ selfish entry choices. Theorem 7highlights the critical role of the relationship between contest-specific abilities in determining whether nudges are welfare enhancing.18Specifically, we find that nudges improve welfare when the positive dependence between the contest-specific abilities is large enough. Intuitively, when having high ability in one contest also implies a high ability in the other contest, the inefficiency in the solver-contest pairings we identified earlier is less severe (because a low/high-ability solver in contest 1 is also likely to be a low/high-ability solver in contest 2). Consequently, the efficiency gain from spreading solvers more evenly dominates, and nudges are welfare enhancing. In contrast, when contest-specific ability dependence is low, then the gain in self-sorting and efficient matching between ability and contest becomes more important, and platform nudges diminish the overall welfare. Theorem 7and the intuition above were formulated in terms of ability correlations. From a practical perspective, it is also of interest to discuss the origin of these correlations and their implications for the platform. Specifically, when two contests are related (or they are in similar domains), we would expect a given solver’s abilities in the two contests to be positively correlated. As a concrete operationalization, suppose that a solver’s ability in contest 1 isa1=λa+(1−λ)ε1for some weightλ∈[0,1], and in contest 2, it isa2=λa+(1−λ)ε2, wherearepresents a “global” ability andεirepresents a contest-specific idiosyncratic ability. When two contests are related (such as two data-science contests), we would expect the weightλon global ability to be larger; whereas when the two contests are unrelated (such as a data-science contest and a logo-design contest), we should expect the weight1−λon contest-specific idiosyncratic ability to be larger. Our result suggests that nudging solvers into specific contests is welfare enhancing when these two contests are related, and allowing solvers to self-sort is appropriate otherwise. The following example illustrates the insight ofTheorem 7for arbitrary marginal distributions and a reasonable set of inter-contest ability dependence. (Nudging Dominates Blind Entry, If and Only If, inter-contest Ability Dependence Exceeds a Threshold). Assume that solver abilities in each contest have quantilesQ1andQ2with joint probability distribution given by the bivariate Frank copula BecauseC(q1,q2;·)is strictly increasing inδ, the same holds for the respective joint CDF, and hence, (Pearson’s) correlation ofQ1,Q2is also strictly increasing inδ. Asδ→0, the Frank copula converges toC(q1,q2;0)≔q1q2(i.e., the case where contest abilities are independent). Asδ→+∞(respectively,δ→−∞), the Frank copula converges to the fully positively (respectively, fully negatively) correlated cases of contest abilities. When we nudge solvers to contests, the expected value of the maximum quantile from each contestjis given byRevjnudged=∫01(1−qn/2)dq=nn+2. Under solver-induced entry, the expected value of the maximum participating quantile in each contestjis given byRevj(δ)=∫01(1−(12+12C(q,q;δ))n)dx, which is continuous and strictly decreasing inδ. Moreover,Revj(0)>Revjnudged, whereaslimδ→∞Revj(δ)=n+2−n−1n+1<Revjnudged. Thus, nudging is beneficial, if and only if, inter-contest dependence (measured by parameterδ) is sufficiently large.
article.7. Concluding Remarks: Much of the prior theoretical literature on contests has studied the case of a single monopolist contest organizer. Modeling contest dynamics on platforms and specifically, the between-firm competition for solvers who can freely self-select which contest to commit resources to is a natural first step, one that is necessary toward our understanding of how solvers and multiple firms interact on a crowdsourcing platform. Our modeling framework captures unique aspects of dueling contests that differ from the extant literature in several important ways. First and perhaps most interesting is the realization that solvers’ endogenous entry creates coordination problems and associated inefficiencies. Our analysis of both noise-driven and ability-driven contests finds that self-sorting into contests imposes specific costs on the firms, the solvers, and the platform; such costs persist for any finite population size. Specifically, solver equilibrium entry per contest might be excessive, wherein solvers end up clustering into a few contests with higher ex ante potential because of a larger budget. This implies that solvers tend to neglect too many of the alternative contests. This appears to make everyone worse off: each individual solver as well as firms’ and solvers’ aggregate welfare. Second, we are able to characterize how a firm should optimally react to competing prize allocations. We find that many structural results from the monopolistic contest literature may be (and may not be) robust to competition. Contest noise, for example, determines a firm’s prizes, but sufficiently noise-driven contests do not benefit by the provision of multiple prizes. This can explain why innovation contests for open-ended problems hosted on crowdsourcing platforms often offer a single winner-takes-all prize. Third, we find that the dependence structure of solver abilities across contests can be an important design parameter for crowdsourcing platforms. Specifically, nudging solvers toward contests is optimal for “focused” platforms hosting contests that require similar skills (e.g., data-science and machine-learning skills). In contrast, platforms for “general” crowdsourcing tasks should instead let solvers self-select contests. The implications of these findings for a crowdsourcing platform are as follows. The crowdsourcing platform should take into careful consideration the contest characteristics organized by firms because modifying the contest budget can improve welfare (for example, by requiring firms to choose among a few available budget choices so that contests appear ex ante similarly attractive to solvers). Moreover, by promoting featured contests to certain solvers, it may be possible for a contest platform to prevent excessive competition between solvers and thus, to improve overall welfare. The authors thank Department Editor of Revenue Management and Market Analytics Gabriel Weintraub for his support throughout the review process. The authors are grateful for the continuing support from the associate editor and the thoughtful and constructive comments from three anonymous reviewers, whose suggestions considerably improved the manuscript. The authors also thank Michael Ostrovsky and Ariel Procaccia as well as the participants of the Twenty-First Association for Computing Machinery Conference on Economics and Computation, the 2019 INFORMS Revenue Management and Pricing Conference at Stanford University, and several other conferences for their valuable inputs on earlier versions of this paper. 1Our main results are qualitatively similar for the case of an oligopoly withm≥3competing firms (see the Online Appendix). Contest design of a single monopolistic contest (with endogenous entry) is a special case of our framework; see Online Appendix EC.5. 2There is no closed-form expression for the equilibrium effort within a unified model with ability, effort, and noise and with a finite number of solvers. Yet, a unique equilibrium effort exists under weak assumptions and, similarly, it is strictly increasing in ability, seeStouras (2017). 3This holds for any contests in which the competitors do not know how many other competitors have chosen to compete (e.g., contests on Wazoku Crowd (previously InnoCentive) and Witmart; also, consider an applicant applying for a faculty position, a researcher submitting for grant funding, or a firm competing for a patent). More fundamentally, besides the competitors, contest designers face significant participation uncertainty at the contest design stage as they cannot deduce with certainty the number of submissions their contestwillreceive eventually over its duration. 4In our framework, contests with low values of contest sensitivityϑare mathematically equivalent to contests with large noise variance, a well-known condition for equilibrium existence with pure-strategy effort in the monopolistic contest literature (see, e.g.,Nalebuff and Stiglitz 1983). 5Ales et al. (2017, proposition 2) identifies log convexity of the noise distribution as a sufficient condition for WTA to be suboptimal, but they do not characterize the optimal prize allocation in this case.Ales et al. (2017)also establish the WTA optimality when the noise density is log concave or increasing. If the latter is satisfied, then the noise distribution belongs to the IFR class, but the converse is not true. 6This modification keeps the number of participants in each contestfixedatn, regardless ofpj. 7Detailed calculations are available from the first author upon request. 8By contrast, in a purely noise-driven monopoly, the choice of prizes is irrelevant as all solvers participate with certainty (without exerting any effort). 9In our framework, a contest with low values ofϑis mathematically equivalent to a noise with large variance. 10Our nudging result holds in general for nonidentical budgets and for an oligopoly ofm≥2firms. 11A similar argument can be made about solver effort as well. For example, when there aren= 2 solvers, the nudged equilibrium efforts are zero in each contest, whereas without nudges, efforts are definitely positive. We treat the general case withn≥3in Proposition EC.3 in the Online Appendix. 12Homogeneous contests (i.e., when solver ability is globally the same across contests (but ability differs between solvers)) are a limiting case of our framework. This corresponds to contests that require very similar or even identical skills; see Online Appendix EC.4. 13By definition, in ability-driven contests—before making his entry decisions—each solver knows his own realizationzijof the noiseZijin each contest. That is,zijis a privately knownconstantfor solveri. However, because of incomplete information, the realized ability of everyothersolverk≠iin contestjis random variables for solverI, and we denote them byZkj. Clearly, solver abilities are random variables for the firms as well. 14This is without loss of generality as there are no other symmetric equilibria. Indeed, by the revenue equivalence theorem, a solver’s expected utility from participating in each contest is a nondecreasing function of his ability in that contest. Thus, we focus on Bayesian–Nash equilibria in which each solver uses a threshold strategy to determine his participation decision in each contest. 15This symmetry trivially holds when contest abilities are independently drawn from the same (global) marginal ability distributionF. 16When budgets are equal and the joint density is asymmetric, thenγ(z)=zis not a solution toEquation (6). Yet, when budgets are unequal, there are (asymmetric) probability densities for whichγ(z)=zsatisfiesEquation (6). 17We thank an anonymous reviewer for suggesting this interesting possibility. 18We should also note that the specific contingency identified inTheorem 7(ii)is by no means unique. Indeed, even when contest-specific abilities are completely independent (i.e., they exhibit no positive dependence), there are specific ability distributions for which nudges are welfare enhancing. We focus on thepositivedependence as it admits an interesting contest-specific operational guideline on when nudges are welfare enhancing.
